_description: Beers Criteria 2023 rules for potentially inappropriate medication use in older adults (age >= 65). Subset focused on medications relevant to SignalLoop demo scenarios.
_source: American Geriatrics Society 2023 Updated AGS Beers Criteria for Potentially Inappropriate Medication Use in Older Adults. J Am Geriatr Soc. 2023;71(7):2052-2077.

criteria.rationale: Increases risk of GI bleeding/peptic ulcer disease and acute kidney injury in older adults. Risk further increased with concurrent use of corticosteroids, anticoagulants, or antiplatelet agents.
criteria.exceptions: Short-term use (< 1 week) may be acceptable with gastroprotection if no renal impairment

criteria.rationale: Risk of C. difficile infection, bone loss, fractures. Use beyond 8 weeks only with clear indication (erosive esophagitis, Barrett's, pathological hypersecretory conditions).
Increased sensitivity in older adults. Increased risk of cognitive impairment, delirium, falls, fractures, motor vehicle crashes.
Highly anticholinergic; clearance reduced with advanced age. Risk of confusion, dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, falls.
Most muscle relaxants poorly tolerated by older adults due to anticholinergic effects, sedation, and risk of falls. Effectiveness at tolerable doses questionable.
Highly anticholinergic, sedating, and cause orthostatic hypotension. Risk of falls, cardiac conduction abnormalities.
Glibenclamide (glyburide) — higher risk of severe prolonged hypoglycaemia in older adults due to long half-life and active metabolites.
